Why are there no black people on ""Sex and the City""?

But that said, what is it that you think African-American women and men who come to your site get in terms of a perspective on the urban, cosmopolitan dating scene that they might not get from places that are not targeted to a black audience?
Wonderful.
I love that question.
A couple different things.
One, I think that people have a very clear view that there are millions of black people in this country, and there's millions of different ways to be black.

I'm one voice among those, and so people who want to come, and just get a view basically of what it's like to be black, and living in New York, and dating and just trying to make my way through the whole like, freelance writing world, and things like that.
You get an inside view of it, and so that's really what it's there to provide.

I also have, like, a resources section for people who want specifics about what are the dating sites that are out there, what are the podcasts that are out there, where are some of the places I can go to get more information about dating?
Just in general as well.
What about dating sites?

I mean in the past, like a decade ago, there was just, like, one or two or three, and now there's bazillions, sometimes farmed out by one company that creates, you know, the people who do J-Date, do a Christian dating site, and a black dating site, etc., etc., etc. I mean, are these sites still as hot as they used to be?
They're still extremely hot.
It's a huge industry.
There are over 900 different types of dating sites, and the revenue pumped into the economy, and the money that they get from making these sites is about 500 million dollars.
So that's a half-a-billion dollar industry, specifically for dating sites.
One of the trends that you'll see now is everyone knows, of course, like, Black People Meet, Match.com, and then some of the more saucy ones like Adult Friend Finder.

But those sites are starting to, kind of, segment, and get more specific, like if you like fishing or if you want to find other people to go to first Fridays events or something like that, you'll find specific dating sites for that.
So you can actually put in whatever your specific interest is, plus dating site, and you can get a very segmented dating site for you.
All right.
Give me - we just have time for one tip for online dating, something you should do or shouldn't do.
One thing you should do is be yourself.
Never ever, ever post any picture that's like five years old and 50 pounds lighter or heavier than you are.
It's like your picture should reflect who you are, and the way that you write your profile should reflect who you are as a person, as well as the person that you're trying to attract for a date.
Things you should not do, never have someone meet you in your home on a first date that you've never met before.
Don't give out any specifics.
I say, don't tell someone where you work unless you want them to come and visit you there.

First, senior diplomatic correspondent Mike Shuster is back at NPR West, just returned from the former Soviet Republic of Georgia.
He went to cover that war with Russia there last month.
It was mercifully brief enough that it was pretty much over by the time he arrived, but the story is not, Mike.

First, you're an eye witness to what Georgia looks like now.
What does it look like?
How bad is the damage?
Actually the truth is, Alex, Georgia looks pretty much the same as it looked before the war, the five-day war with Russia in August.
There is, in fact, very little damage.

The damage primarily is confined to South Ossetia - that region that had broken away from Georgia proper many years ago - and a small area around it, and maybe you could say to the town of Gori, which is just south of South Ossetia.
But the rest of the country, even though there were some Russian attacks, the rest of the country is largely the same.
Systems were still working.
I think it's fair to say that the civilian population of Georgia survived this war quite well.
It was the military and its bases that received the brunt of the Russian onslaught.
And that - the purpose of the Russian onslaught was to destroy the Georgian army, and that's exactly what it did.
Well, let me ask you about the civilian population.
What about civilian casualties?
Because there were big claims about people killed both in South Ossetia and in Georgia.
It seems that those claims on both sides were exaggerated.
I don't think that there are accurate figures yet, fatalities on the Georgian side or the South Ossetian side.
But it is clear that it was far less than what's claimed.
The Russians claimed that 1,500 to 2,000 in South Ossetia had been ethnically cleansed, killed, a genocide, the Russians called it.
It's fairly clear now that under 100 civilians in South Ossetia were killed.
I went to Poti, which is a port on the western border of Georgia on the Black Sea.
The Black Sea port where U.S.
relief was supposed to be coming in.

Let's turn now to an emerging issue in this country - the conflict over rights for LGBT people and those of religious conservatives.
We start in Mississippi, which last week passed the Protecting Freedom of Conscience from Government Discrimination Act.

The law says the state won't punish private businesses or groups with religious objections to providing marriage-related services like catering or photography to same-sex couples or transgender people.
State employees can deny marriage licenses and refuse to perform weddings on religious grounds, but they must find someone else in the office to provide those services.
Opponents say the new measure effectively legalizes discrimination in the state, and they are calling for boycotts.
Mississippi Public Radio's Paul Boger reports.
(Chanting) No hate in our state.
No hate in our state.
For the third time in less than a week, demonstrators rallied on the steps of the Mississippi Capitol in Jackson to protest the state's new law.
But Republican State Rep.
of Gary Chism of Columbus believes any controversy will blow over in due time.
Let them alone and leave us alone.
We are elected down here, just like they are elected up there.
If we want to take this kind of stance, we ought to be left alone to do that.
But after the next few months, it won't be a factor.
